title: Ansätz Expressivity and Optimization in Variational Quantum Simulations of Transverse-field Ising Model Across System Sizes
authors: Ashutosh P. Tripathi, Nilmani Mathur, Vikram Tripathi
abstract: We explore the application of the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) to investigate the ground state properties, particularly the entanglement entropy, of the Transverse Field Ising Model (TFIM) in one, two, and three dimensions, considering systems of up to 27 spins. By benchmarking VQE results against exact diagonalization and analyzing the entanglement properties across different system sizes and geometries, we assess the algorithm's effectiveness in capturing critical phenomena. Using results of TFIM, we also investigate how VQE's expressivity and optimization influence the simulation of highly entangled quantum states. We employ different ansätze: the hardware-efficient EfficientSU2 from Qiskit, the physics-inspired Hamiltonian Variational ansätz (HVA) and HVA with symmetry breaking, and benchmark their performance using energy variance, entanglement entropy, spin correlations, and magnetization. We further discuss the implications for scaling these methods to larger quantum systems.
summary: This paper evaluates the performance of different variational quantum circuit architectures for simulating the Transverse-field Ising Model. By comparing hardware-efficient and physics-inspired ansätze across 1D, 2D, and 3D lattices, the authors reveal a tension between the ease of optimization and the accuracy of captured entanglement. The results demonstrate that while physics-inspired models better capture critical phenomena, they are significantly harder to optimize as dimensionality increases. This work provides essential guidance for designing scalable VQE algorithms for many-body physics.
main_problem: Investigating the trade-off between ansatz expressivity and optimization stability in the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) when simulating quantum phase transitions.
main_result: The study identifies a fundamental trade-off where hardware-efficient ansätze (HEA) provide smoother optimization landscapes but fail to capture entanglement, while physics-inspired ansätze (HVA) achieve higher fidelity but face more rugged optimization landscapes.
method: The authors use the VQE algorithm with different ansätze (HEA, HVA, and HVA-SB) and optimizers (L-BFGS, COBYLA), benchmarking results against Exact Diagonalization and DMRG using GPU-accelerated state-vector simulations.
model_or_system: The Transverse-field Ising Model (TFIM) in 1D, 2D, and 3D dimensions, featuring ferromagnetic interactions and a transverse magnetic field.
key_observables: Ground-state energy, energy variance, von Neumann entanglement entropy, spin correlations, and magnetization.
important_parameters: Transverse field strength (h_x), system size (up to 27 spins), number of ansatz layers, and spatial dimensionality (1D, 2D, 3D).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the chosen parameterized circuits can represent the ground state and that the classical optimizer can reach the global minimum; it operates within NISQ-era constraints.
figures_summary: Figures show VQE circuit structures (HEA vs HVA-SB), distribution of 1-design frame potential for expressivity, energy and entropy signatures of criticality across 1D, 2D, and 3D lattices, and a comparison of VQE performance against ED and DMRG.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the VQE problem and ansätze, details the TFIM model and simulation framework, quantifies ansatz expressivity and resource scaling, presents benchmarking results across dimensions, and concludes with optimization challenges and future directions.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and quantum computing as it provides critical insights into how circuit architecture and optimization strategies impact the ability to simulate phase transitions and entanglement in higher-dimensional quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic of the VQE circuits for HEA (hardware efficient ans¨atz), HVA (Hamiltonian variational ans¨atz) and HVA-SB (symmetry-breaking) used in this work.

Figure 2: Distribution of 1-degree norm for HEA, HVA and HVA-SB ans¨atze for pairs of states generated respectively from two random parameter sets. The frame potential F1 – the average of the 1-degree norm over random parameter sets – is a measure of expressivity. The Hamiltonian is 1D TFIM.
Figure 3: Average energy and the von Neumann entanglement entropy for the ground state of the one dimensional TFIM at different lattice sizes.
Figure 4: Average energy and the von Neumann entanglement entropy for the ground state of the two dimensional TFIM at different lattice sizes.
Figure 5: Average energy and the von Neumann entanglement entropy for the ground state of the three dimensional TFIM at different lattice sizes.
Figure 6: A comparison between ED, DMRG and VQE methods for energy and entanglement entropy
Figure 7: Energy variance vs hx : TFIM 1-D with 10 sites
Figure 8: Spin correlation, and Magnetization for 10 site 1D system
Figure 9: Spin correlation and Entanglement per site calculation for 2D system of 4x4 dimensions
